title: pathscrambler.exe

pathscrambler.exe

pathscrambler.exe

The pathscrambler.exe executable is used to protect sensitive information such as paths, connection strings, logon information and logicals in Uniface assignment files, Java Servlet settings, and the ProcScript open command.

It takes an assignment file, text string, or path definition as input, and returns an assignment file or string in which specific data is encrypted and/or encoded.

pathscrambler.exe {-encrypt AsnFile PublicKey} | {-instr String } | {-infile AsnFile} | {-path " Path "} | {-asn AsnFile} | {-seed "Seed"}

Table 1. Switches
Option Description
-asn AsnFile Obfuscate path specifications in the [PATHS] section of the specified assignment file. (Provided for backward compatibility.)
-encrypt AsnFile PublicKey Encode the [ENCRYPTED_PATHS] section of <asnfile> using <public_key>.
-infile AsnFile Encrypt any strings in the specified assignment file that are delimited by double parentheses: (( and )). This can be used for logical values, and other login or connection strings that must remain secure.
Note: Encryption of section names is not supported.
-instr "String " Encrypt any strings in the specified path that are delimited by double parentheses: (( and )). If the String contains no delimiters, or only one delimiter, the entire text string is encrypted.
-path "Path " Encode the provided path specification. Use this for scrambling paths in WRD settings.
-seed "Seed "

Sets the seed value that the pathscrambler uses to encrypt data when the -infile or -instr options are specified.

The minimum seed length allowed is 16 characters. If the seed length is shorter, the following message is displayed:
Warning: The specified seed is too short. It must be at least 16 characters long.
The maximum seed length allowed is 64 characters. If the seed length exceeds, the following message is displayed:
Warning: The specified seed is too long. It must not be longer than 64 characters.

Description

Pathscrambler was originally designed to obfuscate path specifications used for automatic logon to resources. It has been enhanced several times, first to support encryption of paths as well as other sensitive data, and later to improve security for encrypted data.

Note: As of Uniface 10.3.02.022, the Pathscrambler encryption using -infile and -instr has changed. If you have previously encrypted strings or assignment files, they will continue to work as they did before. However, if you need to make changes in these strings or files, the new encryption has some consequences. For details, see Pathscrambler Compatibility Issues.

To protect path specifications in settings for the Web Request Dispatcher (WRD), use the -path switch to encode the data.

In all other cases, if you need to protect paths, passwords, connector logon strings, or application logicals, it is recommended that you encrypt the data using -infile or -instr.

For more information, see Encrypting Paths and Other Sensitive Data Using PathScrambler.

Using the -encrypt option

With -encrypt option, you can bind your ENCRYPTED_PATHS section with a public key. Once a public key is bound to your ASN section, Uniface will verify every last resource file's certification.

To use it, just specify the ASN file path as first argument, and the public key path as second argument. When using public keys from OpenSSL, make sure that the public key complies to the specifications we have set in Certifying Uniface Applications.

Encryption

Using the -instr and -infile switches, you can encrypt (rather than just encode) path specifications. The -infile switch also enables you to encrypt other sensitive information such as logical values, and login or connection strings that must remain secure. If you use the -seed switch, the encryption will derive the encryption key from the specified seed. Using a custom seed ensures the encryption is more secure for your application.

An exception applies if you encrypt all your assignment files with the same seed. In that case, you can keep a single $seed setting in usys.asn and remove all other $seed settings from the other assignment files.

To mark data for encryption, you must add the encryption delimiters (( and )) around the strings to be encrypted. The encryption delimiters only have meaning for Pathscrambler.

Pathscrambler replaces the encryption delimiters with the decryption delimiters (! and !), which have meaning for all Uniface executables that read assignment files.

For servlet settings in web.xml, it is only possible to encode the demarcated string using -path. In all other cases, using the -instr and -infile switches, Pathscrambler takes input that has been delimited and does the following:

  • Encrypts the delimited strings.
  • Encodes the encrypted data, which turns binary data into printable characters that can be put in a file or ProcScript.
  • Adds a digest to the end of each line that contains encrypted strings. Because the digest is for the complete line, and not just the encrypted data, this ensures that the encrypted line cannot be tampered with in any way. Even adding a space makes the line unusable.

    The digest at the end of the line is placed within double parentheses: (( digest )). Elsewhere in the encrypted file these are treated as characters, they have no meaning.

Each time the same text is encrypted with the Pathscrambler, it generates a different string, so it is not possible to copy an encrypted string and paste it in different sections of the assignment file.

The generated string can be used as the logon parameter of the ProcScript open statement, or with the /log (login) command line switch.

Output

For -asn and -infile, Pathscrambler generates an assignment file AsnFile.enc containing encrypted data. On iSeries, two dots are not supported, so _ENC is appended to the physical file name ASN. You can copy and paste the contents of this file into the application assignment file.

For the -infile option, if the -seed switch is used, the specified seed is included in the encrypted assignment file. It is stored in an encrypted form as a $seed setting at the end of the first [SETTINGS] section. If the [SETTINGS] section does not exist, the $seed setting is added at the end of the file. The original, unencrypted assignment file should not contain a $seed setting in its [SETTINGS] section.

Pathscrambler writes the setting in the following form:

$seed = (!...!)((...))

Do not remove or modify this setting (including spaces), and do not add additional $seed settings. Altering or adding $seed entries will make the file unreadable by Uniface.

For -instr and -path, by default, the encrypted or encoded data is returned to the screen, STDOUT. You can copy and paste it to the open ProcScript statement, the /log (logon) switch, or into a line in the [PATHS] section of an assignment file.

When using -instr to encrypt a logon string for use with /log or open, do not use -seed, because the seed is only available when Uniface logs in to that path. The seed is wiped from memory as soon as the assignment files are read.

If you use -instr and copy the resulting string into an assignment file's [PATHS] section, the entire line must be encrypted with -instr, including the left-hand part ($name =). If this is not done, the hash at the end of the line will not match.

Additionally, if the file you are copying into was encrypted using -infile and -seed, then the same seed must also be specified on the command line after the -instr flag.

To make it easier to copy and paste the encrypted string, you can direct it to a file, for example, by appending >>FileName to the Pathscrambler command.

For more information, see $log and open and [PATHS].

Marking Data for Encryption

To mark the data to be encrypted, add double parentheses (( and )) as start and end delimiters for each string in the assignment file or input text that needs to be encrypted. Note the following:

  • The (( and )) delimiters must be on one physical line. If one logical line is spread over two physical lines with a continuation marker, then this is considered as one physical line.

    However, you can have multiple delimited parts in one line. For example:

    ORA:database|((username))|((password))

  • If the data for encryption contains the delimiter characters ((, )), (! or!), use the caret symbol (^) to escape them. For example: ^(( and ^(!.
  • When using -instr or -path, enclose the string in double quotes on Windows, and single quotes on Linux and Unix. On Linux and Unix the (( and )) pair will otherwise be interpreted by the shell and have unexpected effects. On iSeries single quotes are always required around all parameters to PATHSCRAMB.
  • When using -instr, if you do not include a pair of (( and )) delimiters, then the whole string will be encrypted, as if the whole string was surrounded by a (( )) pair.
  • When delimiting data to be encrypted, do not delimit section headings, such as [SETTINGS], [PATHS], and so on. Scrambling section headings is not supported.
  • An assignment file containing the encryption delimiters (( and )) cannot be used by Uniface executables.

When Pathscrambler is executed, it replaces the encryption delimiters with the decryption delimiters and resolves ^-escaped characters within the delimited text, before encrypting the data and returning the resulting output.

Escaping Literal Characters

Use the caret (^) to escape characters that must be treated as part of the data rather than interpreted by Uniface. The caret can be used to escape any non-alphanumeric and non-whitespace character. This applies to:

  • Encryption or decryption delimiters: (( )), (! or!).

    For example, ^!) will be treated as literal !) and not a decryption end marker.

  • Line continuation characters: %\

    For example, %^\ will be a literal %\ and not a line continuation marker.

  • The caret symbol itself, when it is not followed by an alphanumeric character or whitespace.

    For example, ^^/ will be literal ^/

    Note: There is no need to escape the caret in trigger names such as ^retrieve.
  • Other non-alphanumeric and non-whitespace characters that must be treated as a literals.

It is good practice to always escape delimiter characters that must be treated as literals, but it is not always required. For example:

  • The start delimiter (( has no special meaning inside an encryption delimiter pair, for example ((abc((def)), so you can choose to escape it or not.
  • A start delimiter that is not followed by an end delimiter on the same line is always a literal string, and does not have to be escaped.

The end delimiter )) does have a special meaning when it occurs inside an encryption delimiter pair, so it must be escaped: ((abc^))def)).

Line Continuation

Uniface has a limit of 1023 characters per line. Encryption has a tendency to lengthen the line, which can be a problem on some operating systems or when specifying many parameters.

When the Pathscrambler detects that the length of the line after encryption exceeds 1023 on Windows and Unix, and 228 on iSeries, it writes a line of maximum length minus two, ends that line with %\, and writes the remainder on the next line. If the remainder is still too long it will do the same again. The 1023-character limit applies to the individual lines that make up the whole line, so this provides a useful way of handling lines that are too long as a result of encryption.

If the Pathscrambler is invoked with -infile and it sees lines with a (( delimiter that ends with the %\ marker, it first concatenates the line(s) that follows before doing the encryption. The marker is not put back in the assignment file produced, unless the maximum length of 1023 (or 228) characters is exceeded. Lines containing no((delimiter and ending with a %\ marker are not changed, but are written to the .enc file unchanged.

The same applies for the -asn switch but only for the [PATHS] section. Any lines in that section using the %\ marker are first concatenated before encoding and the %\ marker is not put back. In any other section the lines remain unchanged.

For more information, see Line Continuation in Assignment Files.

Example: Encrypting Assignment Files

Given the following assignment file with encryption delimiters:

; ASN file myapp.asn
[PATHS]
$DB       ORA:database|((username|password))
$REM_DB   TCP:((machine1+port|user|passwd)) + DB2:((database.schema|db2user|db2passwd))

The following command:

pathscrambler -infile D:\uniface\projects\myapp.asn

Generates the assignment file myapp.asn.enc in D:\uniface\projects with contents something like this:

[PATHS]
$DB       ORA:database|(!AY9WoJPqwtjmFMtDVGVjR2cAYV+uhYMTyQuRvCkDhUpgEY=!)
$REM_DB   TCP:(!AcJLdYGkG7hWWuG8BOHD2vBXeQ3oVNdJhoCayGBC1yl9!) + DB2:(!Af46Mb3EmIYtXLOWbH6plKty0Ggz97A3YwzKp8QtjldvaqwniPDKQTugjZ3nqVimXg==!)

Example: Encrypting Assignment Files with /seed flag

Given the following assignment file with encryption delimiters:

; ASN file myapp.asn
[PATHS]
$DB       ORA:database|((username|password))
$REM_DB   TCP:((machine1+port|user|passwd)) + DB2:((database.schema|db2user|db2passwd))

The following command:

pathscrambler /infile D:\uniface\projects\myapp.asn /seed "This is a really secret seed"

Generates the assignment file myapp.asn.enc in D:\uniface\projects with contents similar to the previous example, including the encrypted $seed setting.

[PATHS]
$DB       ORA:database|(!AY9WoJPqwtjmFMtDVGVjR2cAYV+uhYMTyQuRvCkDhUpgEY=!)
$REM_DB   TCP:(!AcJLdYGkG7hWWuG8BOHD2vBXeQ3oVNdJhoCayGBC1yl9!) + DB2:(!Af46Mb3EmIYtXLOWbH6plKty0Ggz97A3YwzKp8QtjldvaqwniPDKQTugjZ3nqVimXg==!)

[SETTINGS]
$seed = (!A0qozeo5hzTKpin0iKcRuSnlTXzG/rJCVudv87bWkKQm!)((AxqDJ5uwb0duLixvk5Vltwc=))

Example: Encoding Paths

  • pathscrambler -asn myasn.asn

    Results in: myasn.asn.enc containing scrambled paths in the [PATHS] section.

  • pathscrambler -path "localhost|UUSR_C1565|PASS-C156|ASV_ACME"

    Returns something like the following in the console window:

    (fC=n]S|kf7J4bBRRQ5NbU~A5JnR<QABPQ}5@IPQ6bABPRh=FU45B)

Pathscrambler Compatibility Issues

Uniface is backwards compatible with older versions of the pathscrambler, but not the other way around.

The pathscrambler is updated from time to time to use a new encryption method. This means when you install a patch or newer version of Uniface, you should not use the new pathscrambler to encrypt assignment files that are to be used by older versions of Uniface. If the new pathscrambler uses a new method of encryption, those older versions of Uniface will not be able to decrypt the encrypted data in those assignment files because these versions do not support the new method yet.

If you have a mix of different Uniface versions in your environment, use the pathscrambler of the oldest Uniface version to encrypt your assignment files. This will work because Uniface is backwards compatible with older pathscramblers.

Any line that is encrypted by the pathscrambler cannot be altered in any way.

This applies to both – lines in assignment files encrypted using the -infile flag and to strings encrypted using -instr.

It is not recommended to use -instr and then copy the result into an assignment file, because this restriction means that if you encrypt a string using -instr, you cannot copy the result as the value of some path in the [PATHS] section of an assignment file. To be able to copy such a string into an assignment file, you must supply the entire line to pathscrambler -instr, including the path name on the far left, such as $ORA. The resulting string you can copy into an assignment file. If that assignment file was previously encrypted using -seed, then that same seed must be specified on the pathscrambler -instr command using the -seed option.

Any string provided for open or /log must be encrypted in its entirety.

As a consequence, it is no longer possible to dynamically construct a database path using separately encrypted database, user, and password elements. You need to encrypt the complete LogonParameters string for the open statement (or the /log (login) command line qualifier).

Instead, you can dynamically construct a database path in ProcScript using the $password function, which encrypts the password, and the $user function, which does not encrypt the data.

Mixed encryption is not supported in a single string, line, or file.

It is not possible to use an older encryption mixed with the new encryption in one line, string, or file.

You cannot mix lines from an old assignment file and a new one. For example, you cannot cut and paste some lines from an older assignment file into a new one, or add new encryption to an old file.

However, you can use the #FILE command to include an old assignment file in a newly encrypted one.

Related Links
  • Encrypting Paths and Other Sensitive Data Using PathScrambler

    Use the Pathscrambler to encrypt logon strings and other sensitive information in assignment files, servlet settings, the open ProcScript command, and the /log (login) command line switch.

  • Pathscrambler

    The Pathscrambler is command-line utility for encrypting sensitive information and values in assignment files and login strings. These may be used in assignment files, servlet settings, the open ProcScript statement and the /log (login) command line switch. Uniface automatically decrypts any data that has been encrypted with the Pathscrambler.

  • Certifying Uniface Applications

    Certifying a deployed Uniface application can safeguard the privacy of your data. This functionality helps you to verify the validity of your application during runtime, making sure that your database connection settings cannot be used with unauthorized applications.

Was this topic helpful?
LikeDislike

Example: Encrypting Assignment Files Given the following assignment file with encryption delimiters: Explain this code ; ASN file myapp.asn [PATHS] $DB ORA:database|((username|password)) $REM_DB TCP:((machine1+port|user|passwd)) + DB2:((database.schema|db2user|db2passwd)) Copy The following command: Explain this code pathscrambler -infile D:\uniface\projects\myapp.asn Copy Generates the assignment file myapp.asn.enc in D:\uniface\projects with contents something like this: Explain this code [PATHS] $DB ORA:database|(!AY9WoJPqwtjmFMtDVGVjR2cAYV+uhYMTyQuRvCkDhUpgEY=!) $REM_DB TCP:(!AcJLdYGkG7hWWuG8BOHD2vBXeQ3oVNdJhoCayGBC1yl9!) + DB2:(!Af46Mb3EmIYtXLOWbH6plKty0Ggz97A3YwzKp8QtjldvaqwniPDKQTugjZ3nqVimXg==!) Copy Example: Encrypting Assignment Files with /seed flag Given the following assignment file with encryption delimiters: Explain this code ; ASN file myapp.asn [PATHS] $DB ORA:database|((username|password)) $REM_DB TCP:((machine1+port|user|passwd)) + DB2:((database.schema|db2user|db2passwd)) Copy The following command: Explain this code pathscrambler /infile D:\uniface\projects\myapp.asn /seed "This is a really secret seed" Copy Generates the assignment file myapp.asn.enc in D:\uniface\projects with contents similar to the previous example, including the encrypted $seed setting. Explain this code [PATHS] $DB ORA:database|(!AY9WoJPqwtjmFMtDVGVjR2cAYV+uhYMTyQuRvCkDhUpgEY=!) $REM_DB TCP:(!AcJLdYGkG7hWWuG8BOHD2vBXeQ3oVNdJhoCayGBC1yl9!) + DB2:(!Af46Mb3EmIYtXLOWbH6plKty0Ggz97A3YwzKp8QtjldvaqwniPDKQTugjZ3nqVimXg==!) [SETTINGS] $seed = (!A0qozeo5hzTKpin0iKcRuSnlTXzG/rJCVudv87bWkKQm!)((AxqDJ5uwb0duLixvk5Vltwc=)) Copy Example: Encoding Paths pathscrambler -asn myasn.asn Results in: myasn.asn.enc containing scrambled paths in the [PATHS] section. pathscrambler -path "localhost|UUSR_C1565|PASS-C156|ASV_ACME" Returns something like the following in the console window: (fC=n]S|kf7J4bBRRQ5NbU~A5JnR<QABPQ}5@IPQ6bABPRh=FU45B) Pathscrambler Compatibility Issues Uniface is backwards compatible with older versions of the pathscrambler, but not the other way around. The pathscrambler is updated from time to time to use a new encryption method. This means when you install a patch or newer version of Uniface, you should not use the new pathscrambler to encrypt assignment files that are to be used by older versions of Uniface. If the new pathscrambler uses a new method of encryption, those older versions of Uniface will not be able to decrypt the encrypted data in those assignment files because these versions do not support the new method yet. If you have a mix of different Uniface versions in your environment, use the pathscrambler of the oldest Uniface version to encrypt your assignment files. This will work because Uniface is backwards compatible with older pathscramblers. Any line that is encrypted by the pathscrambler cannot be altered in any way. This applies to both – lines in assignment files encrypted using the -infile flag and to strings encrypted using -instr . It is not recommended to use -instr and then copy the result into an assignment file, because this restriction means that if you encrypt a string using -instr , you cannot copy the result as the value of some path in the [PATHS] section of an assignment file. To be able to copy such a string into an assignment file, you must supply the entire line to pathscrambler -instr , including the path name on the far left, such as $ORA . The resulting string you can copy into an assignment file. If that assignment file was previously encrypted using -seed , then that same seed must be specified on the pathscrambler -instr command using the -seed option. Any string provided for open or /log must be encrypted in its entirety. As a consequence, it is no longer possible to dynamically construct a database path using separately encrypted database, user, and password elements. You need to encrypt the complete LogonParameters string for the open statement (or the /log (login) command line qualifier). Instead, you can dynamically construct a database path in ProcScript using the $password function, which encrypts the password, and the $user function, which does not encrypt the data. Mixed encryption is not supported in a single string, line, or file. It is not possible to use an older encryption mixed with the new encryption in one line, string, or file. You cannot mix lines from an old assignment file and a new one. For example, you cannot cut and paste some lines from an older assignment file into a new one, or add new encryption to an old file. However, you can use the #FILE command to include an old assignment file in a newly encrypted one. Related Links Encrypting Paths and Other Sensitive Data Using PathScrambler Use the Pathscrambler to encrypt logon strings and other sensitive information in assignment files, servlet settings, the open ProcScript command, and the /log (login) command line switch. Pathscrambler The Pathscrambler is command-line utility for encrypting sensitive information and values in assignment files and login strings. These may be used in assignment files, servlet settings, the open ProcScript statement and the /log (login) command line switch. Uniface automatically decrypts any data that has been encrypted with the Pathscrambler. Certifying Uniface Applications Certifying a deployed Uniface application can safeguard the privacy of your data. This functionality helps you to verify the validity of your application during runtime, making sure that your database connection settings cannot be used with unauthorized applications.
